conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for ZUP. Cost to produce X was 40 ZUP. </my goals>
<reason>
Since I produced this item at a cost of 40 ZUP and I only have 1 unit, I should aim for at least that amount to ensure I break even. However, since this is the only item available, I can be aggressive with the initial offer. Offering a lower price upfront might encourage the buyer to accept quickly rather than waiting for me to increase the price later. A fair starting point is 30 ZUP, leaving room to negotiate upwards.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 30 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
This is the only item available for sale. I’m starting at 30 ZUP to leave room for negotiation while ensuring I at least cover my production costs. Open to reasonable counteroffers, but this is my initial ask.

Instead, I should counter with a fair offer for the single unit I have. Offering 40 ZUP (my break-even) ensures I cover costs, but I can add some incentive to encourage acceptance. A reasonable counteroffer could be **45 ZUP for 1 unit**, which is slightly above my break-even while still competitive given their stated willingness to pay up to 60 ZUP. This keeps the negotiation alive while protecting my interests.
</reason>

<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I appreciate the bulk offer, but unfortunately, I only have 1 unit available. My cost to produce this item was 40 ZUP, so I’m asking for **45 ZUP**—a fair price that accounts for my investment. This is a reasonable counter to your initial offer of 50 ZUP for 2 units, as I can only provide 1.

While this doesn’t include profit, the opponent is making a fair and reasonable counteroffer, acknowledging my cost structure. Since this is the third proposal I’ve made (starting with 30, then 45, and now countering their 40), I must now choose between **accepting** or **rejecting** this trade.

Accepting this trade ensures I recover my full production cost without further negotiation, which is a solid outcome. Rejecting it would likely end the trade without securing any ZUP, which isn’t beneficial for me since I’m only interested in selling this item. The opponent’s offer is at my break-even point, and I have no further proposals left.
</reason>
